condition.qualitative_description: Declining SWB total area (slope < -2 ha/year) or very few small water bodies relative to watershed size indicates inadequate surface storage infrastructure in hilly terrain.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: In perhumid hill states, rainfall is abundant but highly seasonal and spatially variable. Declining surface water body area over time signals that existing storage structures are silting up, falling into disrepair, or were never adequate. A low count of SWBs relative to watershed area indicates an infrastructure gap that prevents capture of monsoon runoff for rabi and dry-spell use. This mirrors findings from hard-rock terrain studies where check dam siltation progressively reduces storage capacity at ~40 cm/year if unmanaged.

condition.qualitative_description: Mean rabi-to-kharif SWB area ratio below 0.4 indicates rapid post-monsoon drying of water bodies, confirming poor storage persistence into the rabi season.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: A low rabi/kharif SWB area ratio means that water bodies that are full during the monsoon dry out rapidly, leaving little surface water available for rabi irrigation or dry-spell supplementation during kharif. In hilly hard-rock terrain, this rapid drying reflects both siltation-reduced capacity and the absence of groundwater connectivity that would sustain base flows. Dashora et al. (2019) demonstrated that siltation reduces check dam storage and infiltration rates, directly undermining the persistence of water availability for agriculture.

condition.qualitative_description: Fewer than 5 cumulative soil and water conservation or irrigation works recorded under MGNREGA indicates historically low investment in water harvesting infrastructure.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: Low MGNREGA SWC investment in hilly NE states indicates that opportunities to build or rehabilitate small water harvesting structures — check dams, diversion weirs, farm ponds — have not been utilised. This amplifies irrigation challenges because the natural runoff from high rainfall cannot be captured and stored for dry-season use. Conversely, a high nrega_swc_count that has not translated into improved SWB area (sig_01) may indicate poor construction quality or lack of maintenance, particularly desilting.

condition.qualitative_description: No named river passing through or adjacent to the MWS, combined with distant cooperative access (>15 km), indicates both limited surface diversion potential and poor institutional access to credit for irrigation investment.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: In hilly NE terrain, the absence of a perennial river eliminates the option of gravity-fed diversion or lift irrigation from a reliable surface source. When this is combined with poor cooperative access — limiting farmers' ability to obtain credit for pump sets, pipes, or micro-irrigation equipment — the irrigation challenge becomes structural rather than merely infrastructural. Marginal and small farmers in remote hill areas face compounded barriers: no nearby water source and no affordable financing pathway for irrigation investment.

condition.qualitative_description: If farmers report that wells or springs have dried up seasonally or that lifting water requires increasingly deep or distant sources, this confirms that groundwater-fed irrigation is unreliable in the hard-rock hilly terrain of NE states, where aquifer storage is limited to shallow weathered zones.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: Well depth data is not available from the framework for this context. However, in hard-rock hilly aquifers of the Eastern Himalayas, groundwater storage is confined to shallow fractured and weathered zones. Seasonal spring and well failure is commonly reported in Sikkim and Arunachal Pradesh, particularly in the pre-monsoon period. This makes groundwater-based irrigation unreliable as a fallback when surface storage infrastructure is inadequate, compounding the irrigation challenge pathway.

confounders.confounder: Meteorological drought reducing water availability despite adequate infrastructure
confounders.how_to_distinguish: Check mean_annual_precipitation_mm and drought_severe_return_period. If precipitation has declined significantly (trend_annual_precipitation_mm < -30 mm/year) or severe droughts recur frequently (drought_severe_return_period < 4 years), drought is a co-driver rather than infrastructure failure alone. In perhumid NE states, true meteorological drought is rare; persistent water scarcity despite high rainfall strongly implicates infrastructure inadequacy rather than climate deficit.

confounders.confounder: Groundwater depletion pathway (over-extraction) rather than surface infrastructure failure
confounders.how_to_distinguish: In the perhumid Eastern Himalayas, groundwater over-extraction is uncommon given low irrigation intensity and high recharge. If swb_count and nrega_swc_count are both adequate but water scarcity persists, investigate whether borewell proliferation or spring catchment degradation (deforestation) is reducing groundwater availability — this would indicate a groundwater stress pathway rather than irrigation infrastructure failure. The irrigation_challenges pathway is confirmed when infrastructure metrics (swb_total_area_ha, swb_count, nrega_swc_count) are low or declining.

missing_variable_questions.question_to_user: Have farmers in this area noticed that wells, springs, or water sources they use for irrigation have become shallower, dried up seasonally, or required them to go further to fetch water compared to 5–10 years ago? If yes, in which season does this typically occur?
missing_variable_questions.how_answer_updates_diagnosis: If farmers confirm seasonal spring or well failure — especially in the pre-monsoon (March–May) period — this strengthens the irrigation challenges diagnosis by confirming that groundwater-fed fallback irrigation is unreliable. If wells are deepening year-round, consider whether a groundwater stress pathway is co-occurring alongside infrastructure failure.

sources.title: Hydrologic and cost benefit analysis at local scale of streambed recharge structures in Rajasthan (India) and their value for securing irrigation water supplies
sources.relevance_note: Demonstrates that siltation of check dams in hard-rock terrain reduces storage capacity and infiltration rates over time, directly undermining irrigation water availability; manual desilting increased recharge by 84%. Benefit-cost ratio of 4.1 supports tank desilting as a recommended intervention.

sources.title: Climate Change 2014 - Synthesis Report
sources.relevance_note: Provides context for increasing climate variability affecting water availability and the need for adaptation in water infrastructure, relevant to seasonal irrigation failure in perhumid hill regions.

sources.title: Global Monthly Water Scarcity: Blue Water Footprints versus Blue Water Availability
sources.relevance_note: Highlights that water scarcity is often seasonal even in high-rainfall regions, supporting the diagnosis that perhumid NE hill states can face irrigation water scarcity during dry months despite high annual rainfall totals.
